sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Thoracic pain is often mechanical, postural, or rib-related, but it has a lower threshold for considering serious pathology than uncomplicated low back pain.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Localised mid-back pain or stiffness
- Pain with rotation, extension, coughing, or deep breathing
- Possible rib or scapular region pain
- Reduced tolerance to sustained postures or manual tasks

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Consider fracture, malignancy, inflammatory disease, pulmonary embolus, aortic pathology, infection, or shingles if the pattern is atypical
- Night pain, fever, unexplained weight loss, major trauma, progressive neurological change, or chest symptoms increase urgency
- Rib or thoracic pain after lifting can still mimic cardiac, respiratory, or abdominal pathology

sections.title: Management - Primary
sections.content:
- Screen vital signs and major red flags rather than assuming a benign mechanical cause
- Position for comfort and support easier breathing if pain is pleuritic
- Encourage urgent medical review if the story is atypical, systemic, or traumatic
- Use simple symptom relief within scope only after serious causes have been considered

sections.title: Management - Advanced
sections.content:
- Perform focused thoracic, rib, and neurological examination where appropriate
- Consider respiratory or cardiac overlap and coordinate medical assessment early when doubt remains
- Begin mobility, posture, and load-modification advice only when serious pathology is not suspected
- Refer for imaging or doctor review when trauma, osteoporosis risk, or persistent unexplained pain is present

sections.title: Additional Notes
sections.content: - Thoracic pain should be treated as a diagnosis of exclusion more often than routine peripheral joint pain
